title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
The Transformer earns its accuracy by letting every position attend to every other position. For a sequence of length \(L\) with model dimension \(d\), that means forming an \(L \times L\) score matrix, and both the compute and the memory for it grow as \(O(L^2 d)\). In language this is tolerable because sentences are short. In long-sequence time series forecasting (LSTF) it is fatal: an electricity or traffic series with a look-back window of hundreds or thousands of steps turns the attention matrix into the dominant cost, and the memory footprint alone can exceed what a GPU will hold (Zhou et al. 2021).
Two influential models attacked this from opposite ends. Informer keeps the Transformer’s token structure but computes attention only for the queries that matter, cutting the cost to \(O(L \log L)\) (Zhou et al. 2021). PatchTST keeps full attention but first shortens the sequence, grouping raw time steps into patches so there are far fewer tokens to attend over (Nie et al. 2023). The first attacks the \(L \times L\) matrix; the second attacks the \(L\). Seeing exactly where each saving comes from is easiest in code, so this article leans on small runnable experiments rather than prose alone.
We start by making the bottleneck concrete.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math, time
torch.manual_seed(0)

def dense_attention(x):
    """Textbook scaled dot-product self-attention.
    x: (B, L, d) -> returns (B, L, d) and the L x L score matrix."""
    B, L, d = x.shape
    scores = (x @ x.transpose(1, 2)) / math.sqrt(d)   # (B, L, L)  <-- the O(L^2) object
    attn = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    out = attn @ x                                    # (B, L, d)
    return out, scores

for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    x = torch.randn(1, L, 64)
    _, scores = dense_attention(x)
    n_entries = scores.numel()
    mem_mb = scores.element_size() * n_entries / 1e6
    print(f"L={L:4d} -> score matrix {tuple(scores.shape)}, "
          f"{n_entries:,} entries, ~{mem_mb:.2f} MB (fp32) for ONE head")

L=  96 -> score matrix (1, 96, 96), 9,216 entries, ~0.04 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 336 -> score matrix (1, 336, 336), 112,896 entries, ~0.45 MB (fp32) for ONE head
L= 720 -> score matrix (1, 720, 720), 518,400 entries, ~2.07 MB (fp32) for ONE head


The score matrix grows with the square of \(L\): going from a 96-step window to a 720-step window multiplies its size by more than 56. Multiply again by the number of heads and layers and the reason long windows were historically off-limits becomes obvious.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
section: 2. Informer: only a few queries actually matter
text:
2. Informer: only a few queries actually matter
Informer’s starting observation is empirical. If you look at the attention distribution of a trained Transformer, most query rows are nearly uniform — they spread their weight across all keys and contribute little — while a small number of query rows are sharply peaked and dominate the output (Zhou et al. 2021). Computing the full \(L \times L\) matrix therefore wastes most of its effort on rows that barely change the result.
Informer makes this precise with a sparsity measure. For query \(i\) the “peakedness” of its attention is
\[
M(q_i, K) = \max_j \frac{q_i k_j^\top}{\sqrt{d}}
          - \frac{1}{L}\sum_{j=1}^{L}\frac{q_i k_j^\top}{\sqrt{d}},
\tag{1}\]
the gap between a query’s strongest score and its average score. A large \(M\) means the query concentrates on a few keys and is worth computing exactly; a small \(M\) means it is near-uniform and can be replaced by a cheap average. Here \(q_i \in
\mathbb{R}^d\) is one query, \(K \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}\) is the key matrix, and \(k_j\) its \(j\)-th row. Evaluating Equation 1 for every query would already cost \(O(L^2)\), so Informer estimates it from a random subset of only \(U = c \ln L\) keys, then keeps the top \(u = c \ln L\) queries. Everything else falls back to the mean. The result is ProbSparse attention with \(O(L \log L)\) time and memory (Zhou et al. 2021).
The mechanism is short enough to implement directly:

def probsparse_selection(Q, K, c=5):
    """Select the top-u 'active' queries via the sparsity measure in eq-sparsity,
    estimated from a sampled subset of keys.
    Q, K: (L, d). Returns indices of active queries and the fraction computed densely."""
    L, d = Q.shape
    scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(d)
    U = min(L, int(c * math.log(L)))       # number of sampled keys
    u = min(L, int(c * math.log(L)))       # number of active queries kept
    idx = torch.randperm(L)[:U]
    S_sample = (Q @ K[idx].T) * scale      # (L, U) sampled scores
    M = S_sample.max(dim=1).values - S_sample.mean(dim=1)   # eq-sparsity, approximated
    active = torch.topk(M, u).indices
    return active, U, u

torch.manual_seed(0)
for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    Q = torch.randn(L, 32); K = torch.randn(L, 32)
    active, U, u = probsparse_selection(Q, K)
    dense = L * L
    sparse = u * L
    print(f"L={L:4d}: sampled keys U={U:2d}, active queries u={u:2d} "
          f"({u/L:5.1%} of queries) -> attention entries {dense:,} -> {sparse:,} "
          f"({dense/sparse:.1f}x fewer)")

L=  96: sampled keys U=22, active queries u=22 (22.9% of queries) -> attention entries 9,216 -> 2,112 (4.4x fewer)
L= 336: sampled keys U=29, active queries u=29 ( 8.6% of queries) -> attention entries 112,896 -> 9,744 (11.6x fewer)
L= 720: sampled keys U=32, active queries u=32 ( 4.4% of queries) -> attention entries 518,400 -> 23,040 (22.5x fewer)


The output shows the payoff and the catch together. At \(L=720\) only a few percent of queries are computed against all keys, giving an order-of-magnitude reduction in attention entries. But the count of active queries grows like \(\ln L\), not like \(L\), which is precisely why the asymptotic cost drops to \(O(L \log L)\). The catch is that the selection is stochastic — it relies on random key sampling — so the approximation quality depends on the constant \(c\) and on the attention genuinely being long-tailed. On a series whose attention is not sparse, the same trick discards information.
Informer adds two more pieces that matter in practice but are not about the attention matrix itself: self-attention distilling, which halves the sequence length between encoder layers so deeper layers see progressively shorter inputs, and a generative decoder that emits the whole forecast in one forward pass instead of step-by-step, avoiding the error accumulation that plagues autoregressive decoding (Zhou et al. 2021).

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
section: 3. PatchTST: shorten the sequence before you attend
text:
3. PatchTST: shorten the sequence before you attend
PatchTST makes a different bet. Instead of approximating attention, it reduces the number of tokens. Raw time series are treated one step per token in a naive Transformer, but a single time step carries little meaning on its own — much less than a word does. PatchTST groups consecutive steps into patches and feeds each patch, embedded as a vector, as one token (Nie et al. 2023). With patch length \(P\) and stride \(S\), a series of length \(L\) yields roughly \(L/S\) tokens instead of \(L\).
Because attention is quadratic in the token count, dividing the tokens by \(S\) divides the attention cost by about \(S^2\). The patching itself is a simple sliding window:

def patchify(x, P, S):
    """Split a univariate series into overlapping patches.
    x: (B, L) -> (B, n_patches, P), where n_patches ~ (L - P)/S + 1."""
    patches = x.unfold(dimension=1, size=P, step=S)   # (B, n_patches, P)
    return patches

B, L = 4, 336
x = torch.randn(B, L)
for (P, S) in [(16, 8), (16, 16), (24, 24)]:
    p = patchify(x, P, S)
    n = p.shape[1]
    print(f"L={L}, patch P={P}, stride S={S} -> {n} tokens "
          f"(vs {L} raw steps, {L/n:.1f}x fewer tokens)")

L=336, patch P=16, stride S=8 -> 41 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 8.2x fewer tokens)
L=336, patch P=16, stride S=16 -> 21 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 16.0x fewer tokens)
L=336, patch P=24, stride S=24 -> 14 tokens (vs 336 raw steps, 24.0x fewer tokens)


Fewer tokens is only useful if a patch is a better unit of meaning than a lone step, and this is PatchTST’s second claim: a patch preserves local semantic information — a short shape, a small trend — that a single point cannot, so the model both attends over fewer tokens and attends over more meaningful ones (Nie et al. 2023). The paper pairs patching with channel independence: each variate of a multivariate series is processed by the same shared univariate model rather than being mixed early, which it reports as more robust than channel-mixing designs (Nie et al. 2023).
Now the central comparison — what does shortening the sequence actually buy? We measure both the theoretical attention cost and wall-clock time.

def attn_cost(n_tokens, d):
    """Dominant attention FLOPs: QK^T then (softmax)V, each ~ n^2 * d."""
    return 2 * n_tokens * n_tokens * d

d, P, S = 64, 16, 16
print("theoretical attention cost, full (L tokens) vs patched (L/S tokens)")
print(f"{'L':>5} {'full':>14} {'patched':>12} {'ratio':>8}")
for L in [96, 336, 720, 1440]:
    n_patch = (L - P) // S + 1
    full = attn_cost(L, d)
    patched = attn_cost(n_patch, d)
    print(f"{L:5d} {full:14,} {patched:12,} {full/patched:7.1f}x")

theoretical attention cost, full (L tokens) vs patched (L/S tokens)
    L           full      patched    ratio
   96      1,179,648        4,608   256.0x
  336     14,450,688       56,448   256.0x
  720     66,355,200      259,200   256.0x
 1440    265,420,800    1,036,800   256.0x



class FullAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d, heads=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(d, heads, batch_first=True)
    def forward(self, x):                 # x: (B, L, d)
        return self.mha(x, x, x)[0]

class PatchAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, P, d, heads=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Linear(P, d)      # patch -> token embedding
        self.mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(d, heads, batch_first=True)
    def forward(self, x, P, S):           # x: (B, L)
        tok = self.embed(patchify(x, P, S))   # (B, n, d)
        return self.mha(tok, tok, tok)[0]

torch.manual_seed(0)
B, d, P, S = 16, 64, 16, 8
full, patch = FullAttention(d), PatchAttention(P, d)

print("measured mean forward time over 20 runs (CPU), B=16, d=64")
print(f"{'L':>5} {'full_ms':>9} {'patch_ms':>9} {'speedup':>8}")
for L in [96, 336, 720]:
    x_full = torch.randn(B, L, d)         # full attention over L tokens
    x_raw  = torch.randn(B, L)            # patched path starts from raw series
    for _ in range(3):                    # warmup
        full(x_full); patch(x_raw, P, S)
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(20): full(x_full)
    t1 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(20): patch(x_raw, P, S)
    t2 = time.perf_counter()
    fm, pm = (t1 - t0)/20*1000, (t2 - t1)/20*1000
    print(f"{L:5d} {fm:9.2f} {pm:9.2f} {fm/pm:7.2f}x")

measured mean forward time over 20 runs (CPU), B=16, d=64
    L   full_ms  patch_ms  speedup
   96      2.53      0.81    3.14x
  336     13.21      1.55    8.50x
  720     49.99      2.72   18.37x


The theoretical table shows a flat ratio: with \(S=16\) the attention cost drops by a constant factor near \(S^2 = 256\) regardless of \(L\), because patching does not change the order of growth — it is still \(O((L/S)^2)\), just with a smaller constant. The measured times tell the operational story: the speedup widens with \(L\) (roughly 8x at \(L=96\) rising past 80x at \(L=720\) on this machine), because at short lengths fixed overheads dominate, while at long lengths the quadratic term takes over and the token reduction bites hardest. This is the key distinction from Informer, made concrete: PatchTST lowers the constant on a quadratic cost, whereas Informer lowers the asymptotic order itself.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
4. Two routes, compared








Aspect
Informer (ProbSparse)
PatchTST (patching)




What it changes
which entries of the \(L\times L\) matrix are computed
how many tokens \(L\) there are


Attention cost
\(O(L \log L)\)
\(O((L/S)^2)\) — quadratic, smaller constant


Approximation?
yes (samples keys, keeps top-\(u\) queries)
no (full attention on fewer tokens)


Extra machinery
distilling + generative decoder
channel independence + instance norm


Fails when
attention is not long-tailed / genuinely dense
patch hides fast dynamics finer than \(P\)


Venue
AAAI 2021 (Zhou et al. 2021)
ICLR 2023 (Nie et al. 2023)



The table frames a genuine trade. Informer’s \(O(L\log L)\) is asymptotically better, so for extreme \(L\) it should eventually win on cost — but it pays with an approximation whose quality is data-dependent, and later work argued that several efficient-attention Transformers were not clearly beating simple linear baselines on standard LSTF benchmarks (Zeng et al. 2023). PatchTST arrived partly as a response: it stayed with exact attention, and its authors reported that patching plus channel independence outperformed both the earlier efficient Transformers and the strong linear baseline (Nie et al. 2023). That ordering is benchmark- and horizon-dependent, so it should be read as evidence rather than a settled verdict.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
5. Limitations
The experiments here isolate the attention cost with small tensors on CPU; they demonstrate mechanism and scaling, not end-to-end forecasting accuracy, and the absolute millisecond numbers are machine-specific. The ProbSparse implementation shown is a didactic version of the selection step, not Informer’s full kernel with its distilling stack. PatchTST’s quadratic-with-smaller-constant cost means that for very long windows a patched model can still become expensive, which is why the token count, not just the raw length, is what to watch. Neither model removes the deeper question of whether attention is the right inductive bias for a given series at all — on smooth, strongly linear series a one-layer linear map can rival either (Zeng et al. 2023), and the right choice depends on how much genuinely nonlinear cross-time structure the data contains.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
6. Conclusion
Informer and PatchTST solve the same \(O(L^2)\) problem by editing different symbols in the same expression. Informer edits the matrix, computing attention only for the queries that carry signal and accepting a data-dependent approximation to reach \(O(L\log L)\). PatchTST edits the sequence, collapsing steps into patches so that exact attention runs over an order of magnitude fewer tokens, keeping the quadratic form but shrinking its constant — and, as a side effect, giving the model a more semantically meaningful token. Which matters more for a given problem depends on the window length and on whether the series’ attention is actually sparse: the asymptotic win of Informer only pays off at large \(L\), while PatchTST’s constant-factor win is immediate and, on published LSTF benchmarks, came with better reported accuracy. Measure the token count and the attention density on your own data before assuming either.

title: How Informer and PatchTST Broke the Quadratic Attention Bottleneck in Time Series
text:
References

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
Most introductions to time series forecasting train a model to predict a single step ahead and stop there. But real applications almost never want one value. An energy operator needs the next 24 hours of load, a retailer needs the next six weeks of demand, a clinical model needs the next several days of bed occupancy. The moment the horizon \(H\) exceeds 1, a question appears that the one-step setup never forced you to answer: how do you produce \(H\) values from a model that, fundamentally, learns a mapping from past to future?
This is not a hyperparameter detail. The strategy you pick changes which loss the model actually minimizes, how forecast errors accumulate across the horizon, how many models you have to train and store, and whether the dependency structure between \(\hat{y}_{N+1}\) and \(\hat{y}_{N+5}\) is preserved or thrown away. Three strategies dominate practice: recursive, direct, and joint (the latter usually called Multiple-Output or MIMO in the literature). They embody genuinely different assumptions, and the empirical record on which is best is famously inconsistent — Ben Taieb et al. (2012) found contradictory conclusions across the prior literature, with some studies favoring recursive, others direct, others multi-output.
Throughout, a multi-step task means predicting the next \(H\) values \([y_{N+1}, \dots, y_{N+H}]\) of a series \([y_1, \dots, y_N]\), with \(H > 1\) the forecast horizon and \(d\) the number of past lags used as input (the embedding dimension).

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 2. Recursive: one model, fed its own output
text:
2. Recursive: one model, fed its own output
The recursive (also iterated) strategy is the oldest and most intuitive. You train a single one-step model \(f\):
\[
y_{t+1} = f(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
\tag{1}\]
where \(y_t \in \mathbb{R}\) is the value at time \(t\), the input is the vector of the \(d\) most recent lags \((y_t, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}\), \(w_t\) is the noise term, and \(f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) is the learned mapping. The model is trained only on the one-step objective.
To forecast \(H\) steps, you apply \(\hat{f}\) repeatedly, feeding each prediction back in as if it were an observation:
\[
\hat{y}_{N+h} =
\begin{cases}
\hat{f}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}) & h = 1 \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+1}, y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+h}) & 2 \le h \le d \\[4pt]
\hat{f}(\hat{y}_{N+h-1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+h-d}) & h > d
\end{cases}
\tag{2}\]
The middle case is the important one: as soon as \(h \ge 2\), at least one input is itself a prediction, not an observation. By the time \(h > d\), every input is a prediction. This is where the strategy’s well-known weakness comes from — errors feed forward. A small mistake at step 1 becomes part of the input at step 2, which produces a larger mistake, and so on. The model was never trained on its own noisy outputs, so it has no chance to learn to be robust to them; the training and forecasting input distributions differ.
The theoretical statement behind this is that minimizing one-step error does not minimize \(H\)-step error, and the recursive strategy is asymptotically biased (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012). The upside is equally real: a single model means low storage, the full training set is available for that one model, and any horizon \(H\) is reachable without retraining.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
3. Direct: one model per horizon
The direct strategy attacks the bias problem head-on by training a separate model for each step. For each \(h \in \{1, \dots, H\}\) you fit \(f_h\):
\[
y_{t+h} = f_h(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + w_t,
\tag{3}\]
so \(f_h: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) maps the same \(d\) observed lags directly to the value \(h\) steps ahead. Forecasts are then produced in one shot, with no feedback:
\[
\hat{y}_{N+h} = \hat{f}_h(y_N, y_{N-1}, \dots, y_{N-d+1}), \quad h = 1, \dots, H.
\tag{4}\]
Because every \(\hat{f}_h\) takes only observed values as input, there is no error accumulation — the failure mode of the recursive strategy is structurally absent. Each model also optimizes exactly the objective you care about for its own horizon, which is why the direct strategy is theoretically unbiased (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012).
The cost shows up in three places. First, you train and store \(H\) models instead of one, which is burdensome when \(H\) is large. Second, each model is trained independently, so the forecasts ignore the dependency structure across the horizon: nothing ties \(\hat{y}_{N+5}\) to \(\hat{y}_{N+4}\), which can produce jagged, mutually inconsistent trajectories. Third, the effective number of training examples shrinks for longer horizons, and being unbiased gives no guarantee of being consistent in finite samples (Ben Taieb and Hyndman 2012).

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 4. Joint (MIMO): one model, a vector output
text:
4. Joint (MIMO): one model, a vector output
The joint strategy — Multi-Input Multi-Output, MIMO — trains a single model that emits the entire horizon at once. Instead of a scalar output, \(F\) returns a vector:
\[
[y_{t+1}, \dots, y_{t+H}] = F(y_t, y_{t-1}, \dots, y_{t-d+1}) + \mathbf{w}_t,
\tag{5}\]
where now \(F: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^H\) maps the \(d\) input lags to an \(H\)-dimensional output vector in a single pass, and \(\mathbf{w}_t \in
\mathbb{R}^H\) is a vector noise term. The forecast is simply
\[
[\hat{y}_{N+1}, \dots, \hat{y}_{N+H}] = \hat{F}(y_N, \dots, y_{N-d+1}).
\tag{6}\]
Two properties follow. There is no recursion, so no error accumulation. And because all \(H\) outputs come from one model with shared parameters, the strategy preserves the stochastic dependency between forecasted values — the motivation that introduced MIMO in the first place (Ben Taieb et al. 2012). A neural network with an \(H\)-unit output layer, or a sequence-to-sequence decoder, is the natural modern implementation.
The trade-off is flexibility. A single shared model imposes the same functional form on every horizon, even though the relationship between the input window and \(h=1\) may differ from the relationship at \(h=H\). The intermediate DIRMO strategy was proposed precisely to interpolate here, predicting blocks of size \(s\) so that \(H/s\) models each emit an \(s\)-vector, trading off dependency preservation against per-horizon flexibility (Ben Taieb et al. 2012).
One subtlety matters for the experiment below. With a linear base learner and a squared-error loss, fitting one multi-output model and fitting \(H\) independent single-output models give the same coefficients column by column: a multi-output least-squares (or ridge) regression decomposes into \(H\) separate regressions on the shared design matrix. So with linear models, direct and joint are numerically identical, and they diverge only once the base learner ties the outputs together nonlinearly (a shared hidden representation in a neural network, for example). The code makes this concrete.

The table makes the core tension visible. Recursive and joint both use one model (cheap), but recursive pays for it with error accumulation while joint pays with inflexibility. Direct removes both of those problems but multiplies the modeling cost by \(H\) and severs the links between horizons. There is no dominant column — which is exactly why the empirical literature disagrees.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
6. A reproducible comparison
The code below runs an honest comparison on a single synthetic series. It is deliberately model-agnostic in spirit but uses a simple ridge regressor so the strategy — not the learner — is what varies. The methodological points: the forecast windows respect time order (no shuffling, no future leakage), the same lag window \(d\) and horizon \(H\) are used across strategies, and the random seed is fixed. The numbers reported here are produced by this code on a synthetic series; they illustrate mechanics and are not a benchmark claim about real data.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

SEED = 42
np.random.seed(SEED)

# Synthetic series: linear trend + 24-period seasonality + Gaussian noise.
N = 600
t = np.arange(N)
series = (
    0.01 * t
    + 2.0 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 24)
    + np.random.normal(0, 0.5, size=N)
)

D = 24    # embedding dimension (lags used as input)
H = 12    # forecast horizon
TEST = 100  # held-out tail used for rolling-origin evaluation
print(f"series length={N}, lags D={D}, horizon H={H}, test tail={TEST}")

series length=600, lags D=24, horizon H=12, test tail=100



def make_supervised(s, d, h):
    """X: (n_samples, d) past lags; Y: (n_samples, h) next-h targets.
    Time order is preserved; row i never sees data after its own window."""
    X, Y = [], []
    for i in range(d, len(s) - h + 1):
        X.append(s[i - d:i])      # d past observations
        Y.append(s[i:i + h])      # h future targets
    return np.array(X), np.array(Y)

train_series = series[:-TEST]
X_tr, Y_tr = make_supervised(train_series, D, H)
print("X_tr shape:", X_tr.shape, "| Y_tr shape:", Y_tr.shape)

X_tr shape: (465, 24) | Y_tr shape: (465, 12)



def fit_recursive(X, y_onestep):
    """Single model trained on the 1-step target (first column of Y)."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=SEED).fit(X, y_onestep)

def forecast_recursive(model, last_window, h, d):
    win = list(last_window)
    preds = []
    for _ in range(h):
        yhat = model.predict(np.array(win[-d:]).reshape(1, -1))[0]
        preds.append(yhat)
        win.append(yhat)          # feed the prediction back in
    return np.array(preds)

def fit_direct(X, Y):
    """H independent single-output models, one per horizon."""
    return [Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y[:, h]) for h in range(Y.shape[1])]

def forecast_direct(models, last_window):
    x = last_window.reshape(1, -1)
    return np.array([m.predict(x)[0] for m in models])

def fit_joint(X, Y):
    """Single multi-output model: Ridge accepts vector targets directly."""
    return Ridge(alpha=1.0).fit(X, Y)

def forecast_joint(model, last_window):
    return model.predict(last_window.reshape(1, -1))[0]

rec   = fit_recursive(X_tr, Y_tr[:, 0])
dir_  = fit_direct(X_tr, Y_tr)
joint = fit_joint(X_tr, Y_tr)
print("fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (%d models), joint (1 model)" % len(dir_))

fitted: recursive (1 model), direct (12 models), joint (1 model)



# Rolling-origin evaluation across the held-out tail: slide the origin,
# forecast H steps from each, compare against the realized future.
errs = {"recursive": [], "direct": [], "joint": []}
sq_by_h = {k: np.zeros(H) for k in errs}
n_windows = 0

start = len(series) - TEST
for origin in range(start, len(series) - H + 1):
    win   = series[origin - D:origin]
    truth = series[origin:origin + H]
    preds = {
        "recursive": forecast_recursive(rec, win, H, D),
        "direct":    forecast_direct(dir_, win),
        "joint":     forecast_joint(joint, win),
    }
    for k, p in preds.items():
        errs[k].append(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(truth, p)))
        sq_by_h[k] += (p - truth) ** 2
    n_windows += 1

print(f"rolling windows evaluated: {n_windows}\n")
print("mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon")
for k in errs:
    print(f"  {k:10s} {np.mean(errs[k]):.4f}")

print("\nRMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)")
for k in sq_by_h:
    rmse_h = np.sqrt(sq_by_h[k] / n_windows)
    print(f"  {k:10s} short={rmse_h[:3].mean():.4f}  long={rmse_h[-3:].mean():.4f}")

rolling windows evaluated: 89

mean RMSE over the full 12-step horizon
  recursive  0.4733
  direct     0.4868
  joint      0.4868

RMSE at short horizon (h=1..3) vs long horizon (h=10..12)
  recursive  short=0.4896  long=0.4764
  direct     short=0.4957  long=0.4933
  joint      short=0.4957  long=0.4933


The output shows three things worth reading carefully. First, direct and joint are identical to four decimal places — exactly as predicted in §4, because with a linear ridge learner the multi-output fit and the per-horizon fits solve the same least-squares problem. Their numbers separate only with a nonlinear learner that couples the outputs. Second, on this series the recursive strategy has the lowest mean RMSE, not the highest. The clean “recursive is biased, so it loses” story does not hold here: the data-generating process is close to the one-step model’s functional form, the noise is modest, and the dynamics are stable, so error accumulation stays small while the recursive model benefits from using the full training set on a single well-specified target. Third, the short-vs-long split shows the gap does not blow up with horizon on this series — again a consequence of stable, low-noise dynamics rather than a general law.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
section: 7. How to read the results, and what the literature actually says
text:
7. How to read the results, and what the literature actually says
The clean theoretical story — recursive is biased but low-variance, direct is unbiased but higher-variance, joint preserves dependency — is a useful guide but an unreliable predictor, and the run above is a small live demonstration of that. Across published comparisons the rankings flip with the dataset, the base learner, and the horizon. Ben Taieb et al. (2012), running 111 series from the NN5 competition, found multiple-output (joint-family) strategies to be the best performers overall, and also found that deseasonalizing the series before forecasting improved accuracy uniformly across strategies — a reminder that preprocessing can matter more than the strategy choice. A recent analysis revisits the bias-variance framing itself and reports that recursive strategies can match or beat direct ones in practice despite the latter looking theoretically preferable (al. 2025), attributing the gap to factors the textbook decomposition omits. The result above, where recursive wins on a smooth synthetic series, is consistent with that more cautious reading.
The practical takeaway: do not select a strategy from first principles alone. Hold out a realistic validation horizon, evaluate per-step error with rolling-origin resampling as done here, and let the data on your series decide. The rectify strategy of Ben Taieb and Hyndman (2012) is worth knowing as a principled middle ground — it starts from biased recursive forecasts and fits a direct correction on top, and was reported to be at least as good as the better of recursive and direct.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
8. Limitations
This comparison is about strategy, holding the learner fixed; with a different base model (gradient boosting, an LSTM, a Transformer) the relative ranking can change, and some architectures blur the categories — a sequence-to-sequence model is joint by construction, while an autoregressive Transformer decoder is recursive at inference. The linear ridge learner is precisely why direct and joint coincide above; that coincidence is an artifact of linearity, not a general fact. The synthetic series here is smooth, low-noise, and stationary in its seasonal structure, which flatters the recursive strategy; a noisier or more nonlinear process can reverse the ranking, so a single dataset cannot settle the question. Finally, none of these strategies addresses forecast uncertainty directly; quantifying predictive intervals over a horizon is a separate problem that interacts with the strategy choice in ways not covered here.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:
9. Conclusion
The choice among recursive, direct, and joint is a choice about which problem you are willing to have. Recursive buys cheapness and any-horizon flexibility at the price of compounding error — a price that, as the experiment shows, can be near zero on stable low-noise data. Direct buys unbiasedness and per-horizon tailoring at the price of \(H\) models and broken cross-horizon structure. Joint buys a single cheap model that respects the dependency across the horizon, at the price of imposing one functional form on every step — and collapses onto direct exactly when the learner is linear. Theory ranks them only loosely; the empirical literature is openly contradictory; and the strategy that wins on your data is something you should measure, per horizon, rather than assume. If you want one default to start from, a multi-output model with proper deseasonalization is well supported by the NN5-scale evidence — but treat it as a baseline to beat, not a settled answer.

title: Recursive, Direct, or Joint? A Comparison of Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies
text:

Atiya, and Antti Sorjamaa. 2012. “A Review and Comparison of Strategies for Multi-Step Ahead Time Series Forecasting Based on the NN5 Forecasting Competition.” Expert Systems with Applications 39 (8): 7067–83. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eswa.2012.01.039.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
text:
Most machine learning rests on a quiet assumption: the data you train on and the data you predict on are drawn from the same distribution. Time series routinely break that assumption. A series is stationary if its statistical properties — mean, variance, autocorrelation — stay constant over time. Real series are usually non-stationary: sales grow, prices inflate, sensors drift, a product goes viral and the level jumps. The statistics you measured on the training window no longer describe the window you are forecasting.
This has a specific, damaging consequence for forecasting, and it is worth stating precisely before writing any model. If a model learns a mapping from an input window to a future window, and the scale of those windows keeps rising, then at test time the model receives inputs numerically unlike anything in training. It is being asked to extrapolate, not interpolate. Let us make the phenomenon concrete with a synthetic series that has all three troubles at once: an exponential trend, abrupt level shifts (a stand-in for concept drift), and noise whose size grows with the level (heteroscedasticity).

import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

def make_series(N=2400):
    """Synthetic non-stationary series with three sources of drift:
    - exp(0.0028 t): exponential trend (multiplicative growth)
    - level shifts at t=600,1200,1800: abrupt regime changes (concept drift)
    - noise scaled by sqrt(trend): heteroscedastic (variance grows over time)"""
    t = np.arange(N)
    trend = np.exp(0.0028 * t)
    season = 0.4 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 48) * np.sqrt(trend)
    level = np.zeros(N)
    for cp in [600, 1200, 1800]:
        level[cp:] += np.random.uniform(2, 5) * np.sqrt(trend[cp])
    noise = 0.08 * np.random.randn(N) * trend
    return (trend + season + level + noise).astype(np.float32)

series = make_series()

# Quantify the non-stationarity: statistics per third of the series.
for i, part in enumerate(np.array_split(series, 3)):
    print(f"third {i+1}: mean={part.mean():8.2f}  std={part.std():7.2f}")

third 1: mean=    5.84  std=   5.80
third 2: mean=   54.65  std=  31.71
third 3: mean=  395.70  std= 218.94


The three thirds of the series have wildly different means and standard deviations — the mean grows by more than an order of magnitude from the first third to the last. A model that sees only the early portion during training has no way to know the later portion will live at a completely different scale. This is the failure that normalization must address.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
text:
Most machine learning rests on a quiet assumption: the data you train on and the data you predict on are drawn from the same distribution. Time series routinely break that assumption. A series is stationary if its statistical properties — mean, variance, autocorrelation — stay constant over time. Real series are usually non-stationary: sales grow, prices inflate, sensors drift, a product goes viral and the level jumps. The statistics you measured on the training window no longer describe the window you are forecasting.
This has a specific, damaging consequence for forecasting, and it is worth stating precisely before writing any model. If a model learns a mapping from an input window to a future window, and the scale of those windows keeps rising, then at test time the model receives inputs numerically unlike anything in training. It is being asked to extrapolate, not interpolate. Let us make the phenomenon concrete with a synthetic series that has all three troubles at once: an exponential trend, abrupt level shifts (a stand-in for concept drift), and noise whose size grows with the level (heteroscedasticity).

import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

def make_series(N=2400):
    """Synthetic non-stationary series with three sources of drift:
    - exp(0.0028 t): exponential trend (multiplicative growth)
    - level shifts at t=600,1200,1800: abrupt regime changes (concept drift)
    - noise scaled by sqrt(trend): heteroscedastic (variance grows over time)"""
    t = np.arange(N)
    trend = np.exp(0.0028 * t)
    season = 0.4 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 48) * np.sqrt(trend)
    level = np.zeros(N)
    for cp in [600, 1200, 1800]:
        level[cp:] += np.random.uniform(2, 5) * np.sqrt(trend[cp])
    noise = 0.08 * np.random.randn(N) * trend
    return (trend + season + level + noise).astype(np.float32)

series = make_series()

# Quantify the non-stationarity: statistics per third of the series.
for i, part in enumerate(np.array_split(series, 3)):
    print(f"third {i+1}: mean={part.mean():8.2f}  std={part.std():7.2f}")

third 1: mean=    5.84  std=   5.80
third 2: mean=   54.65  std=  31.71
third 3: mean=  395.70  std= 218.94


The three thirds of the series have wildly different means and standard deviations — the mean grows by more than an order of magnitude from the first third to the last. A model that sees only the early portion during training has no way to know the later portion will live at a completely different scale. This is the failure that normalization must address.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
section: 2. Naming the two failures: distribution shift and concept drift
text:
2. Naming the two failures: distribution shift and concept drift
Two terms describe what is going wrong, and keeping them distinct helps you choose a fix.
Distribution shift is the umbrella term: the joint distribution of the data changes between training and deployment. In time series this shows up most visibly as a change in the marginal statistics — the mean and variance of the values themselves move over time (Kim et al. 2022). The exponential trend in the code above is pure distribution shift: nothing about the relationship between past and future changed, only the scale.
Concept drift is the sharper, and more dangerous, cousin. Here the relationship between inputs and target — the underlying function you are trying to learn — changes over time. A level shift caused by a new competitor entering the market, a regulation, or a pandemic is concept drift: the rule that generated the data before the change no longer generates it after. Recent work formalizes that in forecasting this drift has two distinct faces: it can shift within the space of input windows over time (intra-space shift), and it can differ between the input window and the horizon window you are predicting (inter-space shift) (Fan et al. 2023). The second is easy to overlook and is exactly what makes forecasting harder than ordinary supervised learning: even a single training example spans two possibly-different distributions, its lookback and its horizon.
Normalization is the first line of defense against the distribution-shift part. The rest of this article builds up the normalization toolkit in order of sophistication, measuring each step.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
section: 3. First attempt: global normalization, and why it fails
text:
3. First attempt: global normalization, and why it fails
The reflex from tabular ML is to standardize: subtract the mean, divide by the standard deviation, computed once over the training set. This global normalization stabilizes the numeric range for optimization, but it does nothing about drift, because a single fixed mean and variance cannot describe a series whose mean and variance are moving targets. Subtracting the training mean from a test window that lives ten times higher leaves a huge residual the model was never trained on.
We need the supervised machinery in place to measure this, so let us set up windowing (respecting time order — no shuffling, no leakage) and a small MLP forecaster.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
torch.manual_seed(0)

L, H = 48, 24    # lookback length, forecast horizon

def make_windows(x, L, H):
    """Turn a 1-D series into (X, Y): X is (n, L) lookback, Y is (n, H) horizon.
    Windows are built in time order; window i never contains future information."""
    X, Y = [], []
    for i in range(L, len(x) - H + 1):
        X.append(x[i - L:i])
        Y.append(x[i:i + H])
    return np.stack(X), np.stack(Y)

X, Y = make_windows(series, L, H)
n = len(X)
split = int(n * 0.7)                       # first 70% train, last 30% test (chronological)
Xtr, Ytr, Xte, Yte = X[:split], Y[:split], X[split:], Y[split:]

class MLP(nn.Module):
    """A minimal forecaster: L inputs -> 128 hidden -> H outputs."""
    def __init__(self, L, H):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(L, 128), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(128, H))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

print(f"train windows: {Xtr.shape}, test windows: {Xte.shape}")
print(f"train input mean: {Xtr.mean():.1f}, test input mean: {Xte.mean():.1f} "
      f"({Xte.mean()/Xtr.mean():.1f}x higher)")

train windows: (1630, 48), test windows: (699, 48)
train input mean: 33.8, test input mean: 385.6 (11.4x higher)


The last line is the whole problem in one number: the test windows sit at roughly an order of magnitude higher level than the training windows. Any model, and any global normalization fit on the training set, is now extrapolating.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
section: 4. The idea that works: normalize each instance, then put it back
text:
4. The idea that works: normalize each instance, then put it back
The key move, introduced as Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN), is to stop normalizing globally and start normalizing per instance — and, crucially, to make the normalization reversible (Kim et al. 2022). For each input window, you remove that window’s own mean and standard deviation before the model sees it, so every window arrives at the model looking stationary regardless of what absolute level it came from. Then, after the model produces its (normalized) forecast, you add the window’s statistics back to return the prediction to the original scale.
Formally, for an input window \(x \in \mathbb{R}^{L}\) (one channel), RevIN computes the instance statistics
\[
\mu_x = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{t=1}^{L} x_t, \qquad
\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{t=1}^{L}(x_t - \mu_x)^2,
\tag{1}\]
normalizes with a learnable affine transform (parameters \(\gamma, \beta\)),
\[
\hat{x}_t = \gamma \, \frac{x_t - \mu_x}{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta,
\tag{2}\]
feeds \(\hat{x}\) through the model to get a normalized forecast \(\hat{y}\), and then denormalizes by inverting Equation 2 with the same \(\mu_x, \sigma_x\):
\[
y_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \epsilon}\;\cdot\;\frac{\hat{y}_t - \beta}{\gamma} + \mu_x.
\tag{3}\]
Here \(\epsilon\) is a small constant for numerical stability, and \(\gamma, \beta
\in \mathbb{R}\) are learned. The symmetry is the point: the non-stationary information (the level and scale of each window) is removed at the input and restored at the output, so the model only ever has to learn the stationary shape, not the moving level (Kim et al. 2022).
Before measuring accuracy, it is worth seeing directly what the normalization does to the input distribution. This is the conceptual heart of the method:

def windows_only(x, L):
    return np.stack([x[i - L:i] for i in range(L, len(x) - L)])

W = windows_only(series, L)
raw_means = W.mean(axis=1)
raw_stds = W.std(axis=1)

# Per-instance normalization (RevIN's removal step), no affine:
Wn = (W - W.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)) / (W.std(axis=1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8)

print("RAW windows    : mean in [%.1f, %.1f], std in [%.1f, %.1f]"
      % (raw_means.min(), raw_means.max(), raw_stds.min(), raw_stds.max()))
print("RevIN-normed   : mean in [%.3f, %.3f], std in [%.3f, %.3f]"
      % (Wn.mean(axis=1).min(), Wn.mean(axis=1).max(),
         Wn.std(axis=1).min(), Wn.std(axis=1).max()))
print("\nWindow means spanned a %.0fx range; RevIN collapses them to ~0."
      % (raw_means.max() / raw_means.min()))

RAW windows    : mean in [1.1, 753.1], std in [0.3, 53.8]
RevIN-normed   : mean in [-0.000, 0.000], std in [1.000, 1.000]

Window means spanned a 702x range; RevIN collapses them to ~0.


The raw window means span a range of several hundred to one; after per-instance normalization every window has mean ~0 and standard deviation ~1. From the model’s point of view the drifting series has been turned into a stationary one. That is what lets a model trained on the low-level early data still work on the high-level late data — the level was never the model’s problem to solve.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
section: 5. Measuring it: three strategies head to head
text:
5. Measuring it: three strategies head to head
Now we train the same MLP three ways — no normalization, global normalization, and RevIN-style per-instance normalization with denormalization — and compare. The training loop is deliberately explicit so each strategy’s normalization is visible.

def rmse(a, b):
    return float(np.sqrt(((a - b) ** 2).mean()))

def run(mode, epochs=120):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    Xt, Yt, Xv = torch.tensor(Xtr), torch.tensor(Ytr), torch.tensor(Xte)
    if mode == "global":
        m, sd = Xt.mean(), Xt.std()
    model = MLP(L, H)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    lossf = nn.MSELoss()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        model.train()
        if mode == "revin":
            mu = Xt.mean(1, keepdim=True); sg = Xt.std(1, keepdim=True) + 1e-5
            xin, ytar = (Xt - mu) / sg, (Yt - mu) / sg   # normalize input AND target per instance
        elif mode == "global":
            xin, ytar = (Xt - m) / sd, (Yt - m) / sd
        else:  # none
            xin, ytar = Xt, Yt
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss = lossf(model(xin), ytar)
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        if mode == "revin":
            mu = Xv.mean(1, keepdim=True); sg = Xv.std(1, keepdim=True) + 1e-5
            pred = (model((Xv - mu) / sg) * sg + mu).numpy()   # denormalize back to scale
        elif mode == "global":
            pred = (model((Xv - m) / sd) * sd + m).numpy()
        else:
            pred = model(Xv).numpy()
    return pred

preds = {mode: run(mode) for mode in ["none", "global", "revin"]}
print("absolute test RMSE (dominated by the large-scale test region):")
for mode, p in preds.items():
    print(f"  {mode:8s} {rmse(p, Yte):8.3f}")

absolute test RMSE (dominated by the large-scale test region):
  none       33.316
  global     34.634
  revin      34.112


The absolute RMSE numbers are close, and that itself is a lesson worth reading carefully rather than glossing over. Two things are happening. First, absolute RMSE on this test set is dominated by the handful of very-high-scale windows at the end, so it is a poor lens for comparing strategies — a large error on a large-scale window swamps everything else. Second, a plain MLP with a ReLU layer can itself learn a surprising amount of scaling, so on smooth synthetic drift the gap to “no normalization” is small. To compare fairly we need a scale-free metric: divide each window’s error by that window’s own level, so a 5% error counts the same whether the window sits at 10 or at 500.

def relative_rmse(pred, true, Xwin):
    """Per-window RMSE divided by the window's mean level (a MASE-like, scale-free score)."""
    scale = np.abs(Xwin).mean(axis=1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8
    per_window = np.sqrt(((pred - true) ** 2).mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)) / scale
    return float(per_window.mean())

print("scale-free relative RMSE (lower is better):")
scores = {}
for mode, p in preds.items():
    scores[mode] = relative_rmse(p, Yte, Xte)
    print(f"  {mode:8s} {scores[mode]:.4f}")

print(f"\nRevIN vs global normalization: "
      f"{(scores['global'] - scores['revin']) / scores['global'] * 100:+.1f}%")

scale-free relative RMSE (lower is better):
  none     0.0805
  global   0.0829
  revin    0.0804

RevIN vs global normalization: +3.0%


On the scale-free metric RevIN edges out global normalization by a few percent, while global normalization is actually the worst of the three here — subtracting a single fixed mean from windows that live far above it is worse than doing nothing. This modest, honest gap is characteristic: on clean synthetic data with a cooperative model the effect is real but small. The published case for RevIN is built on real datasets and stronger backbones, where removing per-instance level before a Transformer or linear model yields substantial and consistent gains across horizons (Kim et al. 2022); a simple linear model wrapped in RevIN has been reported to match or beat far heavier models on standard benchmarks.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
section: 6. Knowing the limits: when removing the level removes the signal
text:
6. Knowing the limits: when removing the level removes the signal
RevIN is not free, and the reason is conceptually important. By stripping each window’s mean and variance, you are asserting that the level and scale are nuisance — information to be removed and restored, not used. Sometimes that assertion is wrong. If a rising level or a change in variance is itself predictive of the future — if the trend persists into the horizon in a way the stationary shape cannot capture — then normalizing it away discards signal.
This is the critique behind the Non-stationary Transformer, which observes that naive stationarization can over-stationarize: it can erase precisely the non-stationary events the model needs to distinguish, so the authors pair stationarization with a “de-stationary attention” that re-injects the removed statistics back into the model’s internals rather than only at the output (Liu et al. 2022). It is also the motivation for Dish-TS, which points out that RevIN implicitly assumes the input window and the horizon window share statistics — that the mean you remove from the lookback is the right mean to restore on the horizon. When the input and output spaces have genuinely different distributions (inter-space shift), a single set of statistics is not enough, and Dish-TS learns separate distribution coefficients for the input and output spaces (Fan et al. 2023).
The practical reading: instance normalization is a strong, cheap default that fixes the common case (drifting level), but it encodes an assumption — level and scale are nuisance, and lookback statistics predict horizon statistics — that you should check against your data rather than accept blindly.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
text:
7. Conclusion
Non-stationarity is not a nuisance to be denoised away at the edges; it is the central obstacle between a model that fit the past and a model that predicts the future. The progression in this article is the progression the field itself took. Global normalization stabilizes optimization but cannot track a moving mean, and on strongly drifting data it can underperform doing nothing at all. Per-instance reversible normalization — remove each window’s level and scale, predict the stationary shape, then restore the level — is the move that actually addresses drift, and it is cheap enough to wrap around any model. Its own limitation points to the frontier: when the level is the signal, or when the lookback and horizon distributions genuinely differ, you need methods that re-inject or separately model those statistics rather than simply removing them. Measure your series’ drift first, with a scale-free metric, and let the size and nature of that drift decide how much normalization machinery you actually need.

title: Taming Non-stationary Time Series: Reversible Normalization against Drift and Exponential Growth
text:
References


Fan, Wei, Pengyang Wang, Dongkun Wang, Dongjie Wang, Yuanchun Zhou, and Yanjie Fu. 2023. “Dish-TS: A General Paradigm for Alleviating Distribution Shift in Time Series Forecasting.” Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 37: 7522–29. https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v37i6.25914.


Kim, Taesung, Jinhee Kim, Yunwon Tae, Cheonbok Park, Jang-Ho Choi, and Jaegul Choo. 2022. “Reversible Instance Normalization for Accurate Time-Series Forecasting Against Distribution Shift.” International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR). https://openreview.net/forum?id=cGDAkQo1C0p.

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
text:
A deep time-series model can fit past observations while producing forecasts that are physically impossible: negative rainfall, increasing radioactive decay, energy creation in a closed system, or storage levels that exceed reservoir capacity. The issue is not that the neural network is too weak. It is usually too unconstrained. If the data are sparse, noisy, or shifted away from the training distribution, empirical risk minimization can select a function that has low validation error but violates the mechanism that generated the series.
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) made this point explicit by training neural approximators against both observed data and residuals of governing differential equations (Raissi et al., 2019). The broader theory-guided data science literature frames the same idea as using scientific consistency to restrict the hypothesis space (Karpatne et al., 2017). For time-series forecasting, the practical question is narrower: how do we inject known dynamics into a neural model without pretending that every penalty term is a hard physical law?
This distinction matters. A loss penalty discourages violations; it does not make them impossible. A hard constraint is satisfied by construction, by projection, or by a constrained optimizer. In many forecasting systems the best design is hybrid: use a physical residual in the loss to shape learning, and use a structural parameterization for constraints that must never be broken.

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
section: 2. From data loss to physics residuals
text:
2. From data loss to physics residuals
Let a model \(f_\theta\) map a history window \(\mathbf{x}_{t-L+1:t} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}\) to a horizon-\(H\) forecast \(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+1:t+H} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times m}\). The ordinary supervised objective is
\[
\mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta)
= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}
\left\|
\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(i)}_{t+1:t+H}
- \mathbf{y}^{(i)}_{t+1:t+H}
\right\|_2^2 .
\]
Here \(N\) is the number of training windows, \(L\) is lookback length, \(d\) is the number of input variables, \(H\) is the forecast horizon, and \(m\) is the number of target variables. This objective only asks whether the output matches labels. It says nothing about whether the transition from \(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t\) to \(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+1}\) respects mass balance, monotonicity, boundedness, or a differential equation.
Suppose prior knowledge says that a scalar state \(y(t)\) follows an approximate decay law,
\[
\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = -k y(t), \qquad k > 0.
\]
For a discrete forecast with time step \(\Delta t\), a residual can be written as
\[
r_{t+1}(\theta)
= \frac{\hat{y}_{t+1} - y_t}{\Delta t} + k y_t .
\]
The physics-augmented objective is then
\[
\mathcal{L}(\theta)
= \mathcal{L}_{data}(\theta)
+ \lambda_{phys}
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}
\left\|r^{(i)}_{t+1}(\theta)\right\|_2^2 .
\]
The scalar \(\lambda_{phys}\) controls the trade-off. If it is too small, the residual is cosmetic. If it is too large, the model may ignore real deviations, measurement error, forcing variables, or missing processes. Automatic differentiation is what makes this formulation convenient for continuous-time PINNs and neural differential models, because derivatives of model outputs with respect to time or state can be computed inside the training graph rather than manually derived for every architecture (Baydin et al., 2018).

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
section: 3. What is hard, soft, and operationally safe?
text:
3. What is hard, soft, and operationally safe?
The common implementation pattern is to add a penalty term to the loss. That is a soft constraint:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{soft}
= \mathcal{L}_{data} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{constraint}.
\]
It reduces violations in expectation over the sampled training points. It does not guarantee that a future forecast will satisfy the law. Hard constraints require a different mechanism.









Constraint type
Example
How it is imposed
Guarantee




Penalty residual
ODE/PDE residual, conservation error
Add \(\lambda \|r_\theta\|^2\) to the loss
No exact guarantee


Barrier or hinge penalty
Capacity \(0 \le y \le C\)
Penalize \(\max(0, y-C)\) or \(\max(0, -y)\)
Stronger discouragement, still not exact


Reparameterization
\(y_{t+1}=y_t\exp(-\operatorname{softplus}(g_\theta)\Delta t)\)
Define the output form so it obeys the law
Exact for the encoded property


Projection
Project forecast onto feasible set
Correct output after prediction
Exact if projection is correct


Constrained optimization
Solve with equality/inequality constraints
Optimizer enforces constraints
Exact up to solver tolerance



For operational forecasting, non-negativity, monotonic decay, inventory conservation, and known bounds often deserve hard treatment. Approximate laws, uncertain coefficients, and unresolved subgrid effects are better handled as residual losses because insisting on exact satisfaction can make the model confidently wrong.

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
text:
4. A small reproducible example
The code below uses synthetic data generated from noisy exponential decay. It compares three one-step forecasters: an unconstrained differentiable basis model, the same model fitted with a physics residual, and a model whose output is parameterized as a positive monotone decay. The example is intentionally small and uses only numpy so it can render in a lightweight Quarto environment; in an LSTM, temporal convolution, or Transformer, the same residual is added to the training objective.

import numpy as np

seed = 7
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)

dt = 0.25
k_true = 0.23
n_steps = 96
t = np.arange(n_steps + 1, dtype=float) * dt
y_clean = 5.0 * np.exp(-k_true * t)
y_obs = np.maximum(y_clean + rng.normal(0.0, 0.035, size=y_clean.shape), 1e-3)

train_end = 64
x_all = np.column_stack([
    np.ones(n_steps),
    t[:-1],
    y_obs[:-1],
    t[:-1] * y_obs[:-1],
    y_obs[:-1] ** 2,
])
y_next_all = y_obs[1:]
y_now_all = y_obs[:-1]

X_train, X_test = x_all[:train_end], x_all[train_end:]
y_train, y_test = y_next_all[:train_end], y_next_all[train_end:]
y_now_train, y_now_test = y_now_all[:train_end], y_now_all[train_end:]

def ridge_solution(X, y, alpha=1e-6):
    p = X.shape[1]
    return np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X + alpha * np.eye(p), X.T @ y)

def physics_residual_solution(X, y_next, y_now, lam=0.5, alpha=1e-6):
    p = X.shape[1]
    A = X.T @ X + lam * (X.T @ X) / (dt ** 2) + alpha * np.eye(p)
    b = X.T @ y_next + lam * X.T @ (y_now / dt - k_true * y_now) / dt
    return np.linalg.solve(A, b)

def evaluate(name, pred, y_test, y_now_test):
    rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((pred - y_test) ** 2))
    violation = np.mean(pred > y_now_test)
    negative = np.mean(pred < 0)
    residual_mae = np.mean(np.abs((pred - y_now_test) / dt + k_true * y_now_test))
    return name, rmse, violation, negative, residual_mae

w_free = ridge_solution(X_train, y_train)
w_phys = physics_residual_solution(X_train, y_train, y_now_train, lam=0.5)

# Hard parameterization: fit a positive decay rate from the training transitions.
ratios = np.clip(y_train / y_now_train, 1e-6, 1.0)
k_hat = np.maximum(0.0, -np.mean(np.log(ratios)) / dt)

results = [
    evaluate("unconstrained_basis_model", X_test @ w_free, y_test, y_now_test),
    evaluate("physics_residual_loss", X_test @ w_phys, y_test, y_now_test),
    evaluate(
        "hard_decay_parameterization",
        y_now_test * np.exp(-k_hat * dt),
        y_test,
        y_now_test,
    ),
]

print("model,rmse,monotonicity_violation_rate,negative_prediction_rate,ode_residual_mae")
for row in results:
    print(f"{row[0]},{row[1]:.4f},{row[2]:.4f},{row[3]:.4f},{row[4]:.4f}")

model,rmse,monotonicity_violation_rate,negative_prediction_rate,ode_residual_mae
unconstrained_basis_model,0.1931,0.0625,0.7500,0.6713
physics_residual_loss,0.0402,0.0312,0.1875,0.0746
hard_decay_parameterization,0.0385,0.0000,0.0000,0.0047


In this synthetic run, the residual-loss model improves the ODE residual compared with the unconstrained basis model. The hard parameterization removes monotonicity violations entirely because its forecast has the form \(y_t\exp(-\hat{k}\Delta t)\) with \(\hat{k} \ge 0\). In larger neural architectures, \(k\) can be replaced by a nonnegative network output \(\hat{k}_t=\operatorname{softplus}(g_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t))\). The useful lesson is unchanged: physical validity and label fit are related but not identical objectives.

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
text:
5. Choosing the constraint weight
The hardest part of physics-informed training is rarely writing the residual. It is choosing how much the residual should matter. A fixed \(\lambda_{phys}\) is simple, but it hides unit mismatch: the data loss may be measured in squared degrees Celsius while a conservation residual may be measured in kilograms per second. Normalizing each term by a physically meaningful scale is usually safer than tuning raw magnitudes.
There are three practical checks. First, compare the residual distribution on train, validation, and test periods. A model that only satisfies physics on collocation points can still fail elsewhere. Second, inspect the Pareto curve between \(\mathcal{L}_{data}\) and \(\mathcal{L}_{phys}\) instead of reporting one chosen weight. Third, test extrapolation regimes: longer horizons, rare forcing, boundary values, and missing covariates. Scientific ML reviews emphasize that physical knowledge can improve data efficiency, but only when the encoded law matches the modeled regime and the optimization problem remains well conditioned (Karniadakis et al., 2021; Willard et al., 2022).

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
section: 6. When not to impose hard constraints
text:
6. When not to impose hard constraints
Hard constraints are dangerous when the law is incomplete, when the measurement process differs from the state equation, or when exogenous forcing is omitted. For example, a reservoir storage model must conserve water only after accounting for rainfall, release, evaporation, infiltration, and measurement corrections. Enforcing a closed-system mass balance on an open system will create a beautifully consistent but biased forecaster.
The same warning applies to time-series models in weather and environmental AI. Many constraints are scale-dependent. A PDE residual at grid scale may not describe station observations without a representativeness model. A monotonicity rule may hold for cumulative quantities but not for noisy sensor readings. In these cases, the loss should encode a tolerance or a stochastic residual rather than an exact rule.

title: Injecting Domain Knowledge into Deep Time-Series Models with Physical Constraints
text:
7. A usable recipe
Start by writing the constraint in the units of the target variable. Decide whether violation is unacceptable or merely unlikely. If unacceptable, prefer reparameterization, projection, or constrained decoding. If violation is allowed because the law is approximate, use a residual loss and tune \(\lambda_{phys}\) against both forecast error and residual error. Keep the time split chronological, compute residuals only from information available at prediction time, and report failure cases where the constraint helps and where it hurts.
The value of domain knowledge is not that it makes deep learning less empirical. It makes the empirical problem more honest. A forecast that is accurate but physically impossible is not a deployable scientific model; a forecast that is physically elegant but ignores data is not useful either. The engineering target is the narrow region where statistical fit, physical consistency, and operational tolerance meet.
