title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text: [Concept Visualization] A diagram summarizing the key concepts of this post.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
section: Where the learning problem actually gets defined
text:
Where the learning problem actually gets defined
For most tabular problems the DataLoader is boilerplate. For time series it is where most of the modelling decisions are quietly made. Choosing the input length fixes how much history the model can see. Choosing the horizon fixes what “prediction” even means. Choosing the stride controls how correlated your samples are. Choosing the split point decides whether the reported test error is honest. None of these decisions live inside nn.Module; they live inside the Dataset and DataLoader.
This article walks through a minimal but realistic pipeline for multivariate, multi-step forecasting on a synthetic series. The goal is not to introduce Dataset or DataLoader — the PyTorch documentation already does that — but to show what each configuration flag actually costs, where data leakage sneaks in, and how to verify the pipeline is doing what you meant it to do.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
section: The API contract, in one sentence
text:
The API contract, in one sentence
A PyTorch Dataset promises two things: it can report its length via __len__, and it can return a single training example on demand via __getitem__(i). A DataLoader is a sampler plus a batcher plus, optionally, a set of worker processes. On each iteration it (i) draws indices from a sampler, (ii) calls dataset[i] for each index, (iii) stacks the returned items into a batch with a collate function, and (iv) hands the batch back to the training loop. Everything else — shuffling, multi-worker prefetch, pinned memory, drop-last — is variations on that theme.
For time series this means the definition of a sample is entirely up to __getitem__. If you write it wrong, no downstream trick will fix it.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
A synthetic multivariate series
To keep the example self-contained we build a three-channel series: one target channel with trend, daily, and weekly components; and two exogenous channels, one loosely and one strongly correlated with the target. Working with a synthetic signal is the only way to know the ground-truth mapping the model is trying to recover.

import numpy as np, torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

torch.manual_seed(0); np.random.seed(0)

def make_series(T=2000, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    t   = np.arange(T)
    base  = 0.002*t + np.sin(2*np.pi*t/96.0) + 0.5*np.sin(2*np.pi*t/24.0)
    x0    = base + rng.normal(0.0, 0.3, size=T)
    x1    = np.cos(2*np.pi*t/168.0) + 0.1*rng.normal(size=T)
    x2    = 0.7*x0 + 0.3*rng.normal(size=T)
    return np.stack([x0, x1, x2], axis=1).astype(np.float32)

series = make_series()
print("series shape:", series.shape, "dtype:", series.dtype)

series shape: (2000, 3) dtype: float32


series shape: (2000, 3) dtype: float32
The shape convention (T, D) — time on the first axis, channels on the second — matches what PyTorch’s recurrent and 1-D convolutional layers expect after batching. The DataLoader will add the leading batch dimension.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
The sliding-window Dataset
A sliding-window dataset turns a long series into a set of (input, horizon) pairs by moving a window along the time axis. The stride argument controls how far the window shifts between consecutive samples. stride=1 yields the maximum number of samples but samples with adjacent starts are almost identical: a batch drawn from them under-represents the distribution and inflates training signal correlations. A larger stride trades sample count for independence, which is often the right trade for long series (Bergmeir and Benı́tez 2012).

class SlidingWindowDataset(Dataset):
    """Wrap a (T, D) numpy array as (L, D) -> (H, C) samples."""
    def __init__(self, series, input_len, horizon, stride=1, target_cols=(0,)):
        assert series.ndim == 2
        self.series      = torch.from_numpy(series)
        self.input_len   = input_len
        self.horizon     = horizon
        self.target_cols = list(target_cols)
        T = series.shape[0]
        self.starts = np.arange(0, T - input_len - horizon + 1,
                                stride, dtype=np.int64)

    def __len__(self): return len(self.starts)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        s = int(self.starts[idx])
        x = self.series[s : s + self.input_len]                                 # (L, D)
        y = self.series[s + self.input_len : s + self.input_len + self.horizon, # (H, C)
                        self.target_cols]
        return x, y

Two design choices are worth naming. First, indices are precomputed at construction time, not derived arithmetically inside __getitem__. This makes it trivial to shuffle, subsample, or filter windows (for example, dropping windows that overlap known outages) without touching the sampling code. Second, the target axis is a list: with target_cols=(0,) the returned y has shape (H, 1), and switching to multi-output forecasting requires only widening the list.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
Splitting without leaking the future
For i.i.d. data a random split is fine. For a time series it is not. Random sampling puts training and test windows on either side of a common point in time, which lets the model interpolate rather than forecast. The correct default is a chronological cut (Hyndman and Athanasopoulos 2021). What is easy to miss is that this cut has to be applied before windowing, and the validation and test segments must include enough leading context so that windows starting near the cut still have a full input history.

T          = series.shape[0]
train_end  = int(T * 0.70)
val_end    = int(T * 0.85)
INPUT_LEN, HORIZON = 96, 24

train_ds = SlidingWindowDataset(series[:train_end],                    INPUT_LEN, HORIZON)
val_ds   = SlidingWindowDataset(series[train_end - INPUT_LEN : val_end], INPUT_LEN, HORIZON)
test_ds  = SlidingWindowDataset(series[val_end   - INPUT_LEN :        ], INPUT_LEN, HORIZON)

print(f"train samples: {len(train_ds)}, val: {len(val_ds)}, test: {len(test_ds)}")
x0, y0 = train_ds[0]
print(f"x shape: {tuple(x0.shape)}, y shape: {tuple(y0.shape)}")

train samples: 1281, val: 277, test: 277
x shape: (96, 3), y shape: (24, 1)


train samples: 1281, val: 277, test: 277
x shape: (96, 3), y shape: (24, 1)
The - INPUT_LEN prefix on the validation and test slices is the small detail that keeps the first validation and test windows valid without ever using future data during training: the training slice ends at train_end, and the validation windows may look backwards into indices before train_end, but they never predict into it.






Figure 1: Sliding windows over the target channel: shaded regions with the same colour are the input (light) and horizon (dark) of a single sample. Adjacent windows share almost all their content when the stride is small.









Figure 2: Chronological split of the raw series: the model never sees future data at training time, and validation windows only consume the leading context needed to form a full input.



Figure 1 makes clear why stride matters: at stride=1, consecutive samples overlap by INPUT_LEN - 1 steps, so a mini-batch of nearby indices is effectively one sample repeated. Figure 2 shows how the split translates to time: the boundaries are hard walls in time, but soft in sample count because of the shared context region.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
What DataLoader actually does
Given a Dataset, a DataLoader produces mini-batches by wrapping a sampler around it and calling a collate function. In the default path this is enough to turn (x, y) tuples of shapes (L, D) and (H, C) into (B, L, D) and (B, H, C) batches without any user code.

train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=True,
                          num_workers=0, drop_last=True)
xb, yb = next(iter(train_loader))
print(f"batch x: {tuple(xb.shape)}, batch y: {tuple(yb.shape)}")

batch x: (64, 96, 3), batch y: (64, 24, 1)


batch x: (64, 96, 3), batch y: (64, 24, 1)
Three flags deserve a comment. shuffle=True samples window start indices uniformly at random each epoch; it does not shuffle within a window, so temporal order inside each sample is preserved. drop_last=True discards the trailing incomplete batch, which stabilizes gradient statistics — with window-based data the last batch can be dramatically smaller than the others. num_workers>0 spawns worker processes that call __getitem__ in parallel; this pays off when __getitem__ is expensive (heavy I/O, augmentation, decoding), and is mostly overhead when the dataset is a lightweight indexed view on an in-memory tensor as it is here.
The next block measures the wall time of two full epochs at three batch sizes, with num_workers=0, to make the trade-offs concrete on a small CPU-bound example.

import time
def bench(batch_size, epochs=2):
    loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True,
                        num_workers=0, drop_last=True)
    for _ in loader: break  # warm-up
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        for xb, yb in loader:
            _ = xb.mean() + yb.mean()
    return time.perf_counter() - t0

for bs in [16, 64, 256]:
    print(f"batch_size={bs}: {bench(bs):.4f} s")

batch_size=16: 0.0764 s
batch_size=64: 0.0218 s
batch_size=256: 0.0249 s


batch_size=16: 0.0283 s
batch_size=64: 0.0225 s
batch_size=256: 0.0495 s






Figure 3: Wall-clock time for two epochs at three batch sizes, single-process. Smaller batches pay Python loop overhead per iteration; larger batches pay memory-copy cost per iteration.



The numbers in Figure 3 are tiny in absolute terms — the dataset is small and lives in RAM — but the pattern is real: throughput is not monotone in batch size. On this run the sweet spot for pure iteration cost is around 64, and larger batches become slower again because each batch copies more data through the collate path. On a GPU with heavy per-example work the picture flips: larger batches usually dominate. The point is that neither answer is universal; the DataLoader configuration has to be measured on the actual workload.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
An end-to-end sanity check
A pipeline is only trustworthy if a simple model trained through it beats a truly trivial baseline. We fit two models: naive persistence, which repeats the last observed target value for the whole horizon; and a single linear map from the flattened input window to the horizon (a direct multi-step head, cf. Salinas et al. (2020) for the more general probabilistic form).

def to_tensors(ds):
    xs, ys = zip(*(ds[i] for i in range(len(ds))))
    return torch.stack(xs), torch.stack(ys)

X_tr, Y_tr = to_tensors(train_ds)
X_te, Y_te = to_tensors(test_ds)

# Baseline A: persistence
last = X_te[:, -1:, 0:1].expand(-1, HORIZON, -1)
rmse_persist = torch.sqrt(((last - Y_te)**2).mean()).item()
mae_persist  = (last - Y_te).abs().mean().item()

# Baseline B: linear direct multi-step
class Linear(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, L, D, H):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc = torch.nn.Linear(L*D, H)
    def forward(self, x):
        b = x.shape[0]
        return self.fc(x.reshape(b, -1)).unsqueeze(-1)

model  = Linear(INPUT_LEN, series.shape[1], HORIZON)
opt    = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_f = torch.nn.MSELoss()
losses = []
loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=128, shuffle=True, drop_last=True)

for epoch in range(8):
    model.train(); epoch_loss = 0.0
    for xb, yb in loader:
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss = loss_f(model(xb), yb)
        loss.backward(); opt.step()
        epoch_loss += loss.item() * xb.size(0)
    losses.append(epoch_loss / len(loader.dataset))
    print(f"epoch {epoch+1}: train MSE = {losses[-1]:.4f}")

with torch.no_grad():
    pred_te = model(X_te)
rmse_lin = torch.sqrt(((pred_te - Y_te)**2).mean()).item()
mae_lin  = (pred_te - Y_te).abs().mean().item()
print(f"persistence  RMSE={rmse_persist:.4f}  MAE={mae_persist:.4f}")
print(f"linear head  RMSE={rmse_lin:.4f}  MAE={mae_lin:.4f}")

epoch 1: train MSE = 1.9627
epoch 2: train MSE = 0.7489
epoch 3: train MSE = 0.4801
epoch 4: train MSE = 0.3027
epoch 5: train MSE = 0.2236
epoch 6: train MSE = 0.1826
epoch 7: train MSE = 0.1609
epoch 8: train MSE = 0.1482
persistence  RMSE=0.9313  MAE=0.7516
linear head  RMSE=0.4251  MAE=0.3398


epoch 1: train MSE = 1.9627
epoch 2: train MSE = 0.7489
epoch 3: train MSE = 0.4801
epoch 4: train MSE = 0.3027
epoch 5: train MSE = 0.2236
epoch 6: train MSE = 0.1826
epoch 7: train MSE = 0.1609
epoch 8: train MSE = 0.1482
persistence  RMSE=0.9313  MAE=0.7516
linear head  RMSE=0.4251  MAE=0.3398






Figure 4: Training MSE per epoch for the linear head, mini-batched through the sliding-window loader.









Figure 5: One test window: the 96-step input on the left, the 24-step ground truth, and the linear model’s 24-step forecast. The prediction tracks the dominant periodicity while under-shooting the extremes.



The persistence baseline gives us a scale for the problem: RMSE 0.93 on the held-out windows just by repeating the last value. A single linear layer, trained through this DataLoader, drops RMSE to 0.43 — a factor of ~2.2 improvement — and MAE to 0.34. That gap is what tells us the pipeline is plumbing the right information through: shuffling is not scrambling time within a window, the chronological split is holding, and the collate is producing the shapes the model expects. Figure 4 shows the loss decreasing monotonically, and Figure 5 shows one test window where the prediction captures the phase and level of the target but not its full amplitude — a typical failure mode of squared-error training on periodic signals.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
Where this pipeline breaks
Three failure modes are worth naming, because they are common and rarely surface as loud errors. The first is silent leakage from a normalization scheme fit on the whole series before the split: fit mean and std on training data only, or use per-window normalization (cf. Hyndman and Athanasopoulos 2021, ch. 3). The second is over-optimistic evaluation when stride=1 and the test set is small: the reported metric is an average over highly correlated windows, and its variance is much larger than the raw sample count suggests; using rolling-origin evaluation (Bergmeir and Benı́tez 2012) gives a more honest picture. The third is memory: for very long series, keeping the entire array in RAM as a tensor is fine, but returning slice views (as SlidingWindowDataset does) instead of copies matters for both speed and footprint — the version above avoids per-item copies because torch.from_numpy(...)[start:stop] is a view.
The DataLoader will not tell you about any of these. The only way to catch them is to sanity-check the pipeline against a trivial baseline before believing anything a deeper model reports.

title: DataLoader, Unpacked: Building a Sliding-Window Pipeline for Time Series in PyTorch
text:
References


Bergmeir, Christoph, and José M. Benı́tez. 2012. “On the Use of Cross-Validation for Time Series Predictor Evaluation.” Information Sciences 191: 192–213. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ins.2011.12.028.


Hyndman, Rob J., and George Athanasopoulos. 2021. Forecasting: Principles and Practice. 3rd ed. OTexts. https://otexts.com/fpp3/.


Salinas, David, Valentin Flunkert, Jan Gasthaus, and Tim Januschowski. 2020. “DeepAR: Probabilistic Forecasting with Autoregressive Recurrent Networks.” International Journal of Forecasting 36 (3): 1181–91. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijforecast.2019.07.001.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:
Ask a forecasting model “how many units will we sell next week?” and a point forecast answers with one number, say 1,000. That number is silent about the question a planner actually has to answer: could it be 700, forcing a stockout, or 1,400, forcing a costly overstock? A point forecast collapses an entire distribution of possible futures into its center, and in doing so throws away the information that every downstream decision — safety stock, staffing, capital reserves, grid capacity — is built on. Probabilistic forecasting keeps that information by predicting a distribution, or a set of quantiles of it, instead of a single value.
The cleanest route to probabilistic forecasts, and the one that underlies modern deep forecasters like the Temporal Fusion Transformer, is quantile regression: directly predict several quantiles of the target’s conditional distribution (Lim et al. 2021). Before any deep learning, the reason this is even possible rests on one loss function from 1978, and understanding it is the whole foundation, so we start there — in code.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:
Ask a forecasting model “how many units will we sell next week?” and a point forecast answers with one number, say 1,000. That number is silent about the question a planner actually has to answer: could it be 700, forcing a stockout, or 1,400, forcing a costly overstock? A point forecast collapses an entire distribution of possible futures into its center, and in doing so throws away the information that every downstream decision — safety stock, staffing, capital reserves, grid capacity — is built on. Probabilistic forecasting keeps that information by predicting a distribution, or a set of quantiles of it, instead of a single value.
The cleanest route to probabilistic forecasts, and the one that underlies modern deep forecasters like the Temporal Fusion Transformer, is quantile regression: directly predict several quantiles of the target’s conditional distribution (Lim et al. 2021). Before any deep learning, the reason this is even possible rests on one loss function from 1978, and understanding it is the whole foundation, so we start there — in code.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
section: 2 The pinball loss, from first principles
text:
2 The pinball loss, from first principles
Ordinary regression minimizes squared error, and the minimizer is the conditional mean. To estimate a conditional quantile instead, we need a loss whose minimizer is the quantile. That loss is the pinball loss (also called the quantile loss or check loss), introduced by Koenker and Bassett (1978). For a target \(y\), a prediction \(\hat{q}\), and a quantile level \(\tau \in (0, 1)\), define the error \(u = y - \hat{q}\) and
\[
\rho_\tau(u) =
\begin{cases}
\tau \, u & u \ge 0 \\
(\tau - 1)\, u & u < 0
\end{cases}
\;=\; \max\big(\tau u,\ (\tau-1)u\big).
\tag{1}\]
The whole idea lives in the asymmetry. When \(\tau = 0.9\), an under-prediction (\(u > 0\), truth above the estimate) is weighted by \(0.9\), while an over-prediction is weighted by only \(0.1\) — so the loss pushes the estimate up until roughly 90% of observations fall below it, which is exactly the definition of the 90th percentile. The key theoretical fact is that the expected pinball loss is minimized at the true quantile \(q_\tau^\ast = F_Y^{-1}(\tau)\) (Koenker and Bassett 1978). Let us verify that claim empirically rather than take it on faith:

import numpy as np
np.random.seed(0)

def pinball(y, q, tau):
    """Pinball loss (eq-pinball) for scalar/array inputs. u = y - q."""
    u = y - q
    return np.maximum(tau * u, (tau - 1) * u)

# Draw samples from a known distribution and find the q that minimizes mean pinball.
sample = np.random.normal(loc=5.0, scale=2.0, size=200_000)

for tau in [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]:
    grid = np.linspace(0, 10, 2001)
    mean_loss = [pinball(sample, q, tau).mean() for q in grid]
    q_hat = grid[int(np.argmin(mean_loss))]
    q_true = np.quantile(sample, tau)      # empirical truth
    print(f"tau={tau}: pinball-minimizing q={q_hat:.3f}  |  true quantile={q_true:.3f}")

tau=0.1: pinball-minimizing q=2.450  |  true quantile=2.451
tau=0.5: pinball-minimizing q=5.010  |  true quantile=5.008
tau=0.9: pinball-minimizing q=7.555  |  true quantile=7.555


The value that minimizes the average pinball loss lands on the empirical quantile for every level. This is not a coincidence to memorize — it is why quantile regression works: swap the squared loss for the pinball loss and gradient descent will chase quantiles instead of the mean. Nothing else about the model has to change.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
section: 3 Why a point forecast plus a constant interval fails
text:
3 Why a point forecast plus a constant interval fails
A common shortcut is to keep a point forecast and bolt on an interval afterward: predict the mean with squared error, measure the residual standard deviation, and draw \(\pm 1.28\sigma\) for an 80% interval assuming normality. This is fast, and on homoscedastic data it is fine. It fails exactly when uncertainty is most interesting — when the spread of the target depends on the input (heteroscedasticity), which is the norm in real series: demand is more volatile during promotions, load more variable in extreme weather.
To show the failure concretely we build a dataset whose noise grows with \(|x|\), then compare two approaches on it. First, the data and the two models.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
torch.manual_seed(0)
np.random.seed(0)

def make_data(N=3000):
    """Heteroscedastic data: the noise scale depends on x, so the *spread*
    of y is a function of the input, not a constant."""
    x = np.random.uniform(-3, 3, N).astype(np.float32)
    sigma = 0.3 + 0.6 * np.abs(x)                       # spread grows with |x|
    y = (np.sin(x) + 0.3 * x + sigma * np.random.randn(N)).astype(np.float32)
    return x, y

x, y = make_data()
xt = torch.tensor(x).unsqueeze(1)
yt = torch.tensor(y).unsqueeze(1)
split = int(len(x) * 0.8)
Xtr, Ytr, Xte, Yte = xt[:split], yt[:split], xt[split:], yt[split:]

QUANTILES = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]

def pinball_loss(pred, target, quantiles):
    """Multi-quantile pinball loss. pred: (B, Q), target: (B, 1).
    Sums eq-pinball over the requested quantile levels."""
    losses = []
    for i, q in enumerate(quantiles):
        e = target[:, 0] - pred[:, i]
        losses.append(torch.max(q * e, (q - 1) * e))
    return torch.stack(losses, dim=1).mean()

class QuantileNet(nn.Module):
    """One network, Q outputs — one per quantile level."""
    def __init__(self, n_q):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(1, 64), nn.ReLU(),
                                 nn.Linear(64, 64), nn.ReLU(),
                                 nn.Linear(64, n_q))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

class PointNet(nn.Module):
    """Ordinary MSE-trained point forecaster (single output)."""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(1, 64), nn.ReLU(),
                                 nn.Linear(64, 64), nn.ReLU(),
                                 nn.Linear(64, 1))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

Now train both: the quantile network on the pinball loss, the point network on MSE, and build the point network’s 80% interval from a single global residual standard deviation.

def train(model, loss_kind, epochs=400, lr=5e-3):
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    mse = nn.MSELoss()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        model.train(); opt.zero_grad()
        out = model(Xtr)
        loss = pinball_loss(out, Ytr, QUANTILES) if loss_kind == "pinball" else mse(out, Ytr)
        loss.backward(); opt.step()
    model.eval()
    return model

qnet = train(QuantileNet(len(QUANTILES)), "pinball")
pnet = train(PointNet(), "mse")

with torch.no_grad():
    P = qnet(Xte).numpy()                       # (n_test, 3): q10, q50, q90
    mu = pnet(Xte).numpy()[:, 0]
    resid = (Ytr.numpy()[:, 0] - pnet(Xtr).detach().numpy()[:, 0])
sigma_global = resid.std()                       # single number for all inputs
lo_pt = mu - 1.2816 * sigma_global               # 80% interval under normality
hi_pt = mu + 1.2816 * sigma_global
Yte_np = Yte.numpy()[:, 0]

print(f"global residual std used by the point model: {sigma_global:.3f}")

global residual std used by the point model: 1.300


The decisive test for any interval is calibration: an interval claiming 80% coverage should actually contain the truth about 80% of the time. And it must do so everywhere, not just on average. We check overall coverage, then split the test set into a low-variance region (small \(|x|\)) and a high-variance region (large \(|x|\)).

xte = Xte.numpy()[:, 0]
low_var = np.abs(xte) < 1.0
high_var = np.abs(xte) > 2.0

def coverage(mask, lo, hi):
    return np.mean((Yte_np[mask] >= lo[mask]) & (Yte_np[mask] <= hi[mask]))

full = np.ones_like(xte, dtype=bool)
print("empirical coverage of nominal 80% intervals (target ~0.80):")
print(f"  quantile   overall={coverage(full, P[:,0], P[:,2]):.3f}  "
      f"low-var={coverage(low_var, P[:,0], P[:,2]):.3f}  "
      f"high-var={coverage(high_var, P[:,0], P[:,2]):.3f}")
print(f"  point+gauss overall={coverage(full, lo_pt, hi_pt):.3f}  "
      f"low-var={coverage(low_var, lo_pt, hi_pt):.3f}  "
      f"high-var={coverage(high_var, lo_pt, hi_pt):.3f}")

print("\naverage 80% interval width:")
print(f"  quantile   low-var={ (P[low_var,2]-P[low_var,0]).mean():.2f}  "
      f"high-var={ (P[high_var,2]-P[high_var,0]).mean():.2f}")
print(f"  point+gauss constant {hi_pt[0]-lo_pt[0]:.2f} everywhere")

empirical coverage of nominal 80% intervals (target ~0.80):
  quantile   overall=0.765  low-var=0.749  high-var=0.737
  point+gauss overall=0.805  low-var=0.985  high-var=0.615

average 80% interval width:
  quantile   low-var=1.51  high-var=4.45
  point+gauss constant 3.33 everywhere


Read the numbers carefully, because they are the whole argument. The point-plus- constant interval looks acceptable overall — close to 80% — but that average hides a dangerous failure. In the low-variance region it covers far more than 80% (a needlessly wide interval, wasting capital on safety stock), and in the high-variance region it covers far less than 80% (a dangerously narrow interval exactly where risk is highest). The quantile model instead widens and narrows its interval with the input — narrow where the data is calm, wide where it is volatile — so coverage stays near target in both regions. A single global \(\sigma\) cannot represent input-dependent uncertainty; quantile regression can, because each quantile is a full function of the input.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
section: 4 Quantile crossing: the one failure mode to watch
text:
4 Quantile crossing: the one failure mode to watch
Fitting each quantile is not quite the end of the story. Because the quantile levels are trained together but nothing structurally forces their order, a model can occasionally output \(\hat{q}_{0.1} > \hat{q}_{0.9}\) — a quantile crossing, which is logically impossible for real quantiles and produces intervals with negative width. It is worth measuring rather than assuming away:

crossing = np.mean((P[:, 0] > P[:, 1]) | (P[:, 1] > P[:, 2]))
print(f"fraction of test points with quantile crossing: {crossing:.4f}")

# The standard cheap fix: sort the predicted quantiles per row.
P_sorted = np.sort(P, axis=1)
crossing_after = np.mean((P_sorted[:, 0] > P_sorted[:, 1]) |
                         (P_sorted[:, 1] > P_sorted[:, 2]))
print(f"after post-hoc sorting: {crossing_after:.4f}")

fraction of test points with quantile crossing: 0.0000
after post-hoc sorting: 0.0000


On this dataset crossing is rare, but on harder problems it is common enough that practitioners either sort the outputs post-hoc (as above), or design the model to predict non-negative increments between consecutive quantiles so the ordering holds by construction — the approach taken by multi-horizon quantile forecasters (Wen et al. 2017). Sorting is a valid, distribution-free repair because reordering quantiles never increases the pinball loss.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:
5 Scoring a whole distribution: CRPS
Coverage checks calibration, but a good probabilistic forecast should also be sharp — as concentrated as possible while staying calibrated. The standard single number that captures both is the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) (Gneiting and Raftery 2007). Its practical charm for quantile models is an identity: the CRPS equals twice the pinball loss integrated uniformly over all quantile levels (Gneiting and Raftery 2007). So the very loss you trained on, averaged across a dense quantile grid, is your proper score. We verify that CRPS rewards a calibrated forecast over an over-dispersed one:

from scipy.stats import norm
np.random.seed(1)

grid = np.linspace(0.01, 0.99, 99)
obs = np.random.randn(20_000)          # truth ~ N(0, 1)

def crps_from_quantiles(pred_q, y):
    """CRPS via the quantile-integral identity: 2 * mean pinball over a uniform grid."""
    total = 0.0
    for i, tau in enumerate(grid):
        total += pinball(y, pred_q[:, i], tau).mean()
    return 2 * total / len(grid)

q_calib = np.array([norm.ppf(g, 0, 1) for g in grid])[None, :].repeat(len(obs), 0)
q_wide  = np.array([norm.ppf(g, 0, 2) for g in grid])[None, :].repeat(len(obs), 0)

print(f"CRPS, calibrated forecast N(0,1)   = {crps_from_quantiles(q_calib, obs):.4f}")
print(f"CRPS, over-dispersed forecast N(0,2) = {crps_from_quantiles(q_wide, obs):.4f}")

CRPS, calibrated forecast N(0,1)   = 0.5698
CRPS, over-dispersed forecast N(0,2) = 0.6622


The calibrated forecast scores lower (better). CRPS penalizes the over-dispersed forecast for being needlessly vague even though a very wide forecast would ace a naive coverage check — which is precisely why you report CRPS and coverage together: coverage guards against overconfidence, CRPS guards against uselessly wide hedging.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
section: 6 From loss to models: DeepAR, TFT, and the two families
text:
6 From loss to models: DeepAR, TFT, and the two families
Everything above uses a trivial MLP so the loss is the only moving part. Real probabilistic forecasters differ in how they turn this machinery into sequence models, and they fall into two families. The quantile-regression family predicts a fixed set of quantiles directly with the pinball loss: the Multi-horizon Quantile Recurrent forecaster established the pattern (Wen et al. 2017), and the Temporal Fusion Transformer made it the output layer of a full attention-based architecture, minimizing the summed quantile loss over levels such as \(\{0.1, 0.5, 0.9\}\) while adding interpretable variable selection and multi-horizon attention (Lim et al. 2021). The parametric family, exemplified by DeepAR, instead has the network output the parameters of a chosen distribution (e.g. the mean and variance of a Gaussian or the parameters of a negative binomial) and trains by maximum likelihood, generating probabilistic forecasts by sampling from the fitted distribution (Salinas et al. 2020).
The trade-off between the families is clean. Quantile regression is distribution-free — it assumes no parametric form, so it adapts to skew and heavy tails — but it only gives you the quantiles you asked for and needs the crossing fix. The parametric approach gives a full, coherent distribution you can sample any statistic from, but only if the distributional family you picked matches reality; the wrong family bakes in a wrong shape. Which to choose depends on whether you can commit to a distributional form for your data.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:
7 Limitations
The experiments here use a single-feature synthetic dataset so the mechanics of each metric are visible; absolute numbers depend on the data and the small model, and a real deployment needs proper time-ordered validation and multi-horizon evaluation rather than the i.i.d. split used for exposition. Quantile regression as shown estimates a fixed grid of quantiles independently and can cross; it also does not, by itself, guarantee calibration on future data under distribution shift, which is where conformal methods that wrap quantile models to give finite-sample coverage guarantees become relevant. Finally, the pinball-based CRPS estimate is only as good as the quantile grid is dense, and none of these scores substitutes for checking that the interval widths are actually actionable for the decision at hand.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:
8 Conclusion
A point forecast answers a question no planner actually asked. The step to probabilistic forecasting is smaller than it looks: replace the squared loss with the pinball loss and a model starts predicting quantiles instead of the mean, with the same architecture and the same optimizer. The payoff is decisive on the data that matters most — where uncertainty depends on the input, a point forecast with a constant interval is simultaneously too wide where the data is calm and too narrow where it is volatile, while quantile regression adapts its interval and stays calibrated across regions. Report coverage and CRPS together, watch for quantile crossing, and choose between the quantile-regression and parametric families based on whether you are willing to commit to a distributional form. Predict the range, not just the number — the range is what the decision needs.

title: Beyond the Point Forecast: Why Quantile Regression Matters for Probabilistic Forecasting
text:

Koenker, Roger, and Gilbert Jr. Bassett. 1978. “Regression Quantiles.” Econometrica 46 (1): 33–50. https://doi.org/10.2307/1913643.
